coredata.dc:title: Determination and the pharmacokinetic study of tigecycline by fluorescence strategy with F, N codoping carbon dots as probe

coredata.dc:description: Tigecycline (TIGE) plays a significant role in the management of complex and serious bacterial infection. The monitoring of TIGE content in plasma and the further application in pharmacokinetic (PK) study is important for clinical medicine, however, the facile and effective strategy is limited. Herein, a sensitive, accurate and facile method for TIGE detection was developed by fluorescence with F, N codoping carbon dots (F, N-CDs) as probe. F, N-CDs exhibited stable maximum emission fluorescence at 500 nm and large Stokes shift of 135 nm. Such property help to weaken the possible self-absorption of biological system and the spectral overlap of fluorophore. Thus, using F, N-CDs as probe, the method exhibited accuracy and similarity of the analytical performance of TIGE detection in human plasma and rat plasma. The validated method was successfully applied to the PK research of TIGE in rat after intravenous injection at three dose levels of 1 μg/g, 2 μg/g, and 4 μg/g at different time-points. The obtained PK parameters of TIGE using this method were basically consistent with current clinical practice. The findings demonstrated the application ability of F, N-CDs for PK study of TIGE ascribed to the clinical need of simple operation, accuracy and rapidity.

head.ce:title: Determination and the pharmacokinetic study of tigecycline by fluorescence strategy with F, N codoping carbon dots as probe

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tigecycline (TIGE) plays a significant role in the management of complex and serious bacterial infection. The monitoring of TIGE content in plasma and the further application in pharmacokinetic (PK) study is important for clinical medicine, however, the facile and effective strategy is limited. Herein, a sensitive, accurate and facile method for TIGE detection was developed by fluorescence with F, N codoping carbon dots (F, N-CDs) as probe. F, N-CDs exhibited stable maximum emission fluorescence at 500 nm and large Stokes shift of 135 nm. Such property help to weaken the possible self-absorption of biological system and the spectral overlap of fluorophore. Thus, using F, N-CDs as probe, the method exhibited accuracy and similarity of the analytical performance of TIGE detection in human plasma and rat plasma. The validated method was successfully applied to the PK research of TIGE in rat after intravenous injection at three dose levels of 1 μg/g, 2 μg/g, and 4 μg/g at different time-points. The obtained PK parameters of TIGE using this method were basically consistent with current clinical practice. The findings demonstrated the application ability of F, N-CDs for PK study of TIGE ascribed to the clinical need of simple operation, accuracy and rapidity.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Fluorine and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: Tigecycline (TIGE), the first glycylcycline antibiotic in clinical application, possesses wide antimicrobial spectrum and strong antimicrobial activity. TIGE has extensive clinical efficacy against the infection caused by common bacteria and drug-resistant bacteria [1,2]. Moreover, TIGE plays a vital role in the treatment of complex and serious bacterial infection in critically ill patients in Intensive Care Unit (ICU). However, U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has warned that TIGE carries a risk of increasing overall mortality [3]. In addition, the excessive use of TIGE can pose a serious threat to human health, including carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic effects [4]. Therefore, in order to ensure the safety and efficacy of the drug, the blood concentration of TIGE should be closely monitored for the individualized and precise medication. Pharmacokinetics (PK), the study of the absorption, distribution, metabolism, and clearance of variable drugs in body on the basis of the monitoring of blood drug concentration, is significant for the clinical individualized precise medication. Usually, severe patients in ICU may encounter large and rapid changes in pathophysiological state, multiple organ dysfunction, hypoproteinemia, and the combination of special treatment methods (such as continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) and extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO). The variable and unpredictable condition of severe patients may lead to individual differences in PK of drugs in patients, making it difficult to predict blood drug concentrations in the case of empirical drug use, and increasing the risk of adverse drug reactions [5]. Therefore, an appropriate blood drug concentration detection technology for TIGE is needed for real-time therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) and future PK study of TIGE. Such strategy can timely and effectively help to adjust the treatment regimens, optimize the antimicrobial treatment, and realize the individualized and accurate medication of anti-infection treatment for severe patients to improve the prognosis based on the use of TIGE.

To date, various kinds of techniques such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering [3], immunochromatography [4], ultra-performance liquid chromatography [6] and liquid-chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [2,7], have been proposed for the determination of TIGE. Nonetheless, most of them usually involve some unavoidable drawbacks of the complexity of sample pretreatment, the high cost of instruments, and the time-consuming detection process, which limit their extensive use in clinic. Benefiting from its convenience, sensitivity, acceptable detection cost and low environmental requirements, fluorescence analysis technology with different sensing principles is extensively concerned for pharmaceutical analysis [8,9]. As a non-fluorescent substance, TIGE can be tested through fluorescence strategies on the basis of derivation [10] and photochemical induction [11]. Moreover, fluorescence / colorimetry method based on CdTe quantum dots and gold nanoparticles was fabricated for TIGE detection [12]. However, the requirement of complicated operation conditions or the possibility of the biological risk restricts the clinical prospect of reported fluorescent methods. Although several studies of biopharmaceutical analysis were achieved through the combination of fluorescence technology and chromatographic approach [13–15], there are still few reports on blood drug monitor for PK study by fluorescence analysis. Admittedly, due to the possible overlapping of emission / excitation spectra ascribed to the small Stokes shift and the absorption of luminescence signals by complex biological intrinsic components, fluorescent probes may suffer photodamage ascribed to living organisms and the auto-fluorescence of the biological matrix. It should be pointed out that the design of appropriate fluorescence probe with specific emission property and Stokes shift may be an effective routine to achieve enhanced biological prospect with the reduced interference from biological background [16].

Compared with organic dyes and inorganic semiconductor quantum dots, because of the optical properties, biocompatibility, and stability, carbon dots (CDs) have been widely studied and applied in the fields of sensing strategies, biological imaging, and drug delivery [17,18]. Among them, the achievement of the modulated photoluminescence of CDs has been the focus of researchers [18,19]. Recently, CDs with longer emission wavelength and suitable Stokes shift are the attractive points for CDs in biosensor and biomedical applications [20–22]. It has been reported that the luminescence properties of CDs can be modulated by variable sources [23], post-treatment through acid / base [24,25], surface functionalization [26] and heteroatom doping [27–29]. Among them, heteroatom doping is one of the most effective methods to improve and regulate the physicochemical and optical properties of CDs. For example, nitrogen (N) doped carbon fluorescent dots prepared by solvothermal method with large Stokes shift (106 nm) were applied to effective detection and imaging [30,31]. While, only N doping or halogen doping seems to have a relatively weak effect on the modulation of the photoluminescence of CDs [32]. In general, the strong electronegativity of elements N and halogen increases the degree of the separation of positive and negative charges by strongly absorbing adjacent electrons [33]. Correspondingly, the co-doping of N and halogen is expected to promote the luminescence properties of CDs with the red-shift wavelength emission and enlarged Stokes shift to alleviate the interference of biological samples for the monitoring of significant drugs in clinic.

Considering the importance of blood concentration monitoring and PK research of TIGE to clinical work, the advantages of fluorescence analysis, and the modulation of photoluminescence (PL) characteristics of CDs by heteroatom doping, the following idea was put forward in this work, as shown in Scheme 1. Fluorine (F) and N co-doped carbon dots (F, N-CDs) were effectively prepared by hydrothermal method using tetrafluoroterephthalic acid as the main source. F, N-CDs exhibit maximum emission fluorescence at 500 nm with a large Stokes shift (135 nm). The interaction and sensitive quenching of F, N-CDs by introduced TIGE can be applied for the detection of TIGE with a low experimental detection limit and wide testing range. Meanwhile, TIGE is not combined with other tetracyclines in clinical practice [34], which avoids the interference of other tetracyclines with this method in real blood samples for the needed specificity. This method had been used to detect TIGE in blood sample with satisfactory recovery and reproducibility. Furthermore, the PK characteristic of TIGE in rat was preliminarily investigated by this method through the monitoring of TIGE in plasma at different time points with different dose degrees. In addition, the obtained PK parameters of TIGE in rat using F, N-CDs as probe suggested the clinical practicability of this method for further PK study of drug.

Tetrafluoroterephthalic acid, tetraethylenepentamine, tigecycline (TIGE), polymyxin B sulfate (PMB) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4.2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4.12H2O), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), L-cysteine (L-Cys), L-arginine (L-Arg) and L-ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), lactose (LAC) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Meropenem for injection (MEM, 0.5 g) was provided by Sumitomo Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Suzhou, China). Cefoperazone sodium for injection (SCF, 1.5 g) was provided by Pfizer Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. Isepamicin sulfate injection (IPM, 2 mL: 200 mg) was provided by Nagoya pharmaceutical factory of Asahi Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. TIGE for injection (TIGE, 50 mg) was produced by Hunan Sailong Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). All aqueous solution used in this work were prepared in ultrapure water from a Millipore system. All reagents with analytical grade were used without any purification.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Decay curves were performed on a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, Britain). Ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were measured on a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was collected using a NICOLET iS50 Infrared Spectroscopy (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were performed on a FEI Talos F200S (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was carried out on Rigaku Ultima IV XRD with Cu-Kα radiation (Rigaku Corporation, Japan).

Briefly, 0.2381 g tetrafluoroterephthalic acid and 5 mL tetraethylenepentamine were mixed with 15 mL ultrapure water by continuous stirring to make it a homogeneous solution. Then, the mixture was transferred to 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180 ℃ for 24 h. After reaction, the solution was cooled to room temperature naturally and further purified through dialysis for 48 h. After freeze-drying, a stock solution of F, N-CDs with a concentration of 2.4 mg/mL was prepared and stored at 4 ℃.

The cytotoxicity of F, N-CDs was determined by standard MTT assay. Normal human hepatocytes (L-02) were inoculated in 96-well plates with a density of 6 × 103 cell/well. After 24 h of culture, 100 μL F, N-CDs (0, 60, 120, 240, 360, 480, 600, 720, 840 μg/mL) were mixed with RPMI-1640 medium and added into the cells for further culture. After 24 h culture, the cells were cultured in medium containing 0.5 mg/mL MTT for another 4 h. Finally, 150 μL DMSO was used to replace the medium. The 96-well plate was oscillated at room temperature for 15 min and the optical density (OD) was determined at 490 nm using a spectrophotometer.

The potential hemolysis ability of F, N-CDs was tested to evaluate the feasibility of the application of F, N-CDs in blood system. Approved by the Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University (Fuzhou, China), fresh human blood was obtained from healthy volunteers with informed consent. Erythrocytes from human blood were collected through standard centrifugation procedure, and then re-dispersed in 1 mL normal saline. Afterwards, the cells were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 15 min. Such centrifugation was performed three times, and then 400 μL erythrocytes were diluted in 9600 μL of normal saline. In normal saline, 2% erythrocyte suspension in normal saline was interacted and incubated with variable F, N-CDs (0, 5, 10, 50, 100, 200 and 500 μg/mL) at 37 ℃ for 3 h. Finally, the erythrocyte suspension was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The absorbance of the supernatant at a wavelength of 540 nm was measured, and the hemolysis rate was calculated. 2% erythrocyte suspension in normal saline and sterilized water were used as negative control and positive control, respectively. The operation was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing performance of TIGE in buffer and plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the clinical practice of TIGE, several common interfering substances in blood and the common clinical drugs used in combination with TIGE were selected for specificity evaluation. The 2 mM Glu, Fru, Suc, KCl, NaCl, MgCl2, Na2CO3, L-Cys, L-Arg, AA, GSH, Lac, 4 mg/mL MEM, SCF, IPM, PMB, and 25 μg/mL TIGE were mixed with 240 μg/mL F, N-CDs for fluorescence record. In addition, the above-mentioned interferences were respectively added to the system of 240 μg/mL F, N-CDs and 25 μg/mL TIGE to compare the fluorescence response to further evaluate the anti-interfering ability of this method for TIGE detection.

In 200 μL pH 7.4 phosphate buffered saline (PBS), 240 μg/mL F, N-CDs was respectively mixed with various concentrations of standard TIGE at 37 ℃ for 30 min for the scan of fluorescence spectra. Moreover, the response of F, N-CDs to various TIGE in diluted human plasma (1%) with PBS (pH 7.4) was also investigated according to the similar procedure in pure PBS.

Herein, the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) obtained from the fluorescence spectra was applied as a criterion for the quantification. F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs at 500 nm in the absence and presence of TIGE, respectively. The system of the presence of F, N-CDs and TIGE was named as F, N-CDs+TIGE in this work. The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the 375–600 nm with 365 nm excitation.

The contents of TIGE for injection (Hunan Sailong Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd., specification: 50 mg, production batch No.: 210219115) under the item of quantity difference were accurately weighed, dissolved with normal saline and quantitatively diluted to make about 0.5 mg in each 1 mL solution as the test solution of TIGE for injection. Then, the content of TIGE in the test solution was determined by this method. Correspondingly, the label amount of TIGE in TIGE for injection was calculated according to the tested result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on the PK of TIGE in rats
ce:section.ce:para: The animal experiment was approved by the Experimental Animal Ethics Committee of Fujian Medical University (Fuzhou, China), and the operation was carried out in accordance with the “Animal Experiment Guide” (No. FJMU IACUC2021-NSFC-0021). All clean Sprague–Dawley rats (referred to as "rats") in the experiment were purchased from the Experimental Animal Center of Fujian Medical University (Production license No.: SCXK (min) 2016–0002).

A male rat weighing 250 ± 20 g was randomly selected for the establishment of the standard curve for TIGE in rat plasma. Blood samples were obtained from the tail vein of the rat at one time. The collected blood samples were put into EDTA anticoagulant tube, centrifuged immediately to obtain plasma, and stored at 4 ℃. In 200 μL diluted rat plasma (10%) with PBS (pH 7.4), F, N-CDs (240 μg/mL) was respectively mixed with various concentrations of standard TIGE. Then, the fluorescence spectra of F, N-CDs+TIGE in rat plasma were monitored according to the same operation condition of that in PBS. Every sample was tested in triple. And the relative standard deviation (RSD) of each TIGE concentration was evaluated to assess the reproducibility of this method in rat plasma.

The PK study of TIGE in rats was carried out as follows: 15 male rats weighing 250 ± 20 g were randomly divided into three groups of low, medium and high dose. The dosage of TIGE injection was 1, 2 and 4 μg/g based on weight in the groups, respectively. The 5 rats in each group were weighed, marked and raised separately. During the experiment, 0.3–0.5 mL of blood was obtained from the severed tail blood of rat as a blank control group before administration. After a single injection of TIGE through the tail vein in the low, medium and high dose groups, 0.3–0.5 mL of fresh blood was collected at different time points (5, 15, 30, 60, 120, 180, 360, 540 and 720 min) in EDTA anticoagulant tube. Then, the blood was centrifuged immediately (3000 rpm for 10 min) to take the plasma for further testing. The fluorescence emission spectra of F, N-CDs with rat plasma samples containing TIGE were recorded in the same procedure as TIGE detection. The TIGE concentration at each time point was obtained according to the standard curve obtained from rat plasma. Then, the PK parameters of TIGE were calculated.

All rats did not receive any drug treatment before the trial, and were adaptively fed at a constant temperature of 25 ± 2 ℃ and humidity of 40–70% for 3 days. During the experiment, rats ate normally and drank freely. At the end of the experiment, all rats were euthanized.

The morphology, molecular structure and optical properties of F, N-CDs were characterized. TEM result ( Fig. 1A) suggested that F, N-CDs were quasi-spherical with good dispersion and uniformity. The size of F, N-CDs distributed in the range of 2.1–7.4 nm with the average diameter of 4.2 nm. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of F, N-CDs (the upper inset in Fig. 1A) showed a broad diffraction peak centered at 21.8°, corresponding to nearly amorphous nature of F, N-CDs [35,36]. Moreover, no additional diffraction peak of F, N-CDs was observed, indicating the acceptable purity of F, N-CDs [37]. FTIR was applied to evaluate the molecular functional groups of F, N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1B. The absorption peaks at 3433 cm-1 and 2829 cm-1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H and C-H, respectively. The typical absorption bands at 1604 cm-1 and 1738 cm-1 can be considered as the stretching vibration of CO and C-H aromatic carboxylic acid [8,38], indicating the presence of hydrophilic -COOH on the surface. In addition, the peaks at 1079 cm-1, 1403 cm-1 and 1357 cm-1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of aromatic C-F, C-N and amide bond [39,40], respectively, implying the successful doping of F and N. Furthermore, the elemental composition of F, N-CDs was analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1 C-G). As shown in Fig. 1C, there were four typical peaks at 284.97 eV、398.92 eV、531.04 eV and 685.63 eV attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s and F1s, respectively. The corresponding atomic contents were 69.56% (C), 16.74% (N), 9.85% (O) and 3.85% (F). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) illustrated three fitting bands of C-C/CC (284.7 eV), C-N/C-O (285.6 eV) and CO/C-F (287.5 eV) [41]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) could be deconvoluted into the binding energies of 398.9 eV (N-H), 399.9 eV (C-N) and 401.8 eV (graphitic nitrogen) [41,42]. In addition, the two fitting bands of O1s (Fig. 1F) were CO (532.2 eV) and C-OH/C-O-C (531.0 eV) [43]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of F1s (Fig. 1G) revealed the characteristic peak of C-F at 685.7 eV [41], suggesting the covalently bonding interaction between elemental C and F. Combined with FTIR analysis and XPS results, it was confirmed that F, N-CDs prepared by one-step solvothermal method were co-doped by F and N with the presence of -OH, -COOH and amide bond to improve dispersion of F, N-CDs in water.

The luminescence characteristics of F, N-CDs were studied. The F, N-CDs emitted the maximum peaks from 491 nm to 542 nm under excitation from 340 nm to 440 nm (Fig. 1H). This result exhibited the similar excitation-dependent luminescence behavior to that of other reported CDs [44–46], which may be attributed to the variable sizes of F, N-CDs (Fig. 1A) [46–49]. F, N-CDs displayed absorption peaks at 225 nm and 365 nm (Fig. 1I), which were attributed to π-π* electronic transition of the sp2 hybrid carbon atom with CC and n-π* electronic transition of CO [50], respectively. The inner illustration in Fig. 1I showed that the canary yellow F, N-CDs solution exhibited strong and stable green fluorescence under UV light. Moreover, the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of F, N-CDs were 365 nm and 500 nm, respectively. And the Stokes shift exhibited the large feature of 135 nm. Increased Stokes shift can effectively reduce the overlap between excitation peak and emission peak with lower self-absorption and improved anti-interference ability for fluorescent probe. The large Stokes shift suggested that F, N-CDs had enhanced potential in biological application through fluorescent strategy. The relative quantum yield of F, N-CDs (Ex = 365 nm, QYF, N-CDs) was evaluated by using quinine sulfate (Ex = 350 nm, QYQS = 54.6%) as reference. According to Fig. S1 and Eq. S1, QYF, N-CDs was calculated to be 5.71%, implying the acceptable luminescent property of the F, N-CDs.

Furthermore, the optical stability of F, N-CDs in a variety of conditions was evaluated (Fig. S2). In the pH range of 3–12, the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs retained above 98%, indicating the stable property of F, N-CDs to pH (Fig. S2A). Furthermore, the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs were almost kept in the modulated conditions of variable temperature (5–95 ℃, Fig. S2B) and salt (0–2000 mM, Fig. S2C). In addition, the photostability of F, N-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. S2D, compared with the commercial organic dyes (FAM and ROX), the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs kept above 92% exposed to 300 W xenon lamp for 90 min, while the fluorescence intensity of FAM and ROX were sharply decreased. Moreover, the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs were kept under the continuous exciting at 365 nm for 1 h (Fig. S2E) and storage at 4 ℃ for 120 days (Fig. S2F), indicating the excellent photobleaching resistance and luminescence stability of F, N-CDs. The successful co-doping of fluorine and nitrogen may improve the optical properties of F, N-CDs [51,52].

Considering the potential biological application, the biocompatibility of F, N-CDs to normal human hepatocytes (L-02) was evaluated using MTT assay, as shown in Fig. S3. Compared with the untreated group, the survival rate of L-02 cells cultured with F, N-CDs (60–840 µg/mL) for 24 h was higher than 70%. It showed that F, N-CDs had little negative effect on L-02 cell, suggesting the low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility of F, N-CDs for biological application. Furthermore, in vitro hemolysis test was used to verify the blood compatibility of F, N-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, erythrocytes ruptured in sterilized water ascribed to the difference in osmotic pressure between inside and outside cells. While, erythrocytes were well dispersed in physiological saline containing different concentrations (5–500 μg/mL) of F, N-CDs. Compared with the negative control group (physiological saline), the hemolytic rate of erythrocytes caused by 500 μg/mL F, N-CDs was only 0.56% (< 5%) (Fig. 2B). The results showed that F, N-CDs dissolved in physiological saline did not cause erythrocyte hemolysis. In addition, with the decrease of the content of F, N-CDs, the hemolysis rate decreased, suggesting the ignorable effect of F, N-CDs to erythrocytes for the drug concentration monitoring in blood.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility and mechanism of TIGE detection using F, N-CDs as probe
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3A, F, N-CDs were quenched with the addition of TIGE, and the quenching degree was enhanced with the increasing concentrations of TIGE. Moreover, the appearance of F, N-CDs solution changed from colorless to yellow after the addition of TIGE, and the green fluorescence of F, N-CDs decreased obviously with UV excitation. The results showed that it was feasible to construct sensitive fluorescence strategy with F, N-CDs as probe through signal-off mode for TIGE detection.

The quenching mechanism of TIGE on F, N-CDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3B, the fluorescence lifetime of F, N-CDs and F, N-CDs+TIGE were 3.5248 ns and 3.5244 ns, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime of quenched F, N-CDs by TIGE almost did not change. It showed that there was no dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between F, N-CDs and TIGE [8]. The UV–vis spectrum of TIGE showed the characteristic absorption peaks of 246 nm and 348 nm, which overlapped with the excitation spectrum of F, N-CDs (Fig. 3C). Generally, the sufficient overlapping of the absorption band of an absorber with the excitation and/or emission bands of a fluorophore may cause inner filter effect (IFE) [8,53]. Quenching efficiency (QE) corrections were made by the consideration of the cell geometry (Fig. 3D) and Equation S2 (shown in Supplementary Material) [54]. Table S1 summarized the absorbance and fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of TIGE. And the corrected fluorescent intensity (Fcor) calculated from Equation S2 at each concentration of TIGE differed greatly. The observed QE was significantly different from that of corrected QE with the different concentrations of TIGE (Fig. 3E), suggesting the presence of IFE of TIGE on F, N-CDs. Moreover, the suppressed efficiency (SE) of IFE for TIGE to F, N-CDs reached as high as 90.53% of the total SE (the calculation method refers to Equation S3) [54], illustrating the main quenching mode of IFE of the quenching process of F, N-CDs by TIGE. Then, the Stern-Volmer curve (Fig. 3F) illustrated that the fluorescence quenching constant (K) decreased with the increased temperature from 25 ℃ to 50 ℃. This phenomenon showed that the newly formed non-fluorescent complex was unstable and decomposed with the increase of temperatures, suggesting the presence of static quenching effect (SQE) between TIGE and F, N-CDs in the testing system [55]. The above results showed the dual effect of IFE and SQE of the quenching mechanism of TIGE on F, N-CDs.

The experimental conditions of this strategy were optimized (Fig. S4). Fig. S4A showed that the change of pH affected the QE of TIGE to F, N-CDs. With the increase of pH, the QE of TIGE to F, N-CDs increased initially and then kept with a little fluctuation. And the QE was highest at pH = 7.4. Furthermore, the reaction temperature and time (Fig. S4B, Fig. S4C) had little effect on QE of TIGE to F, N-CDs in the evaluating ranges. Thus, taking the real biological application into consideration, the optimal reaction temperature and time of F, N-CDs+TIGE were selected as 37 ℃ and 30 min with the simulated physiological environment of pH 7.4.

Clinically severe patients are threatened by drug-resistant Acinetobacter baumannii or Enterobacteriaceae bacterial infection. In the treatment of such severe infected patients, TIGE is a commonly used drug, while other tetracycline antibiotics are not applied. In general, TIGE is used with one or two of other drugs in clinical severe infection, such as carbapenems (such as meropenem, imipenem, etc.), compound preparations of β-lactam antibiotics / β-lactamase inhibitors (such as cefoperazone / sulbactam, etc.), aminoglycosides (such as amikacin, isopamicin, etc.) and mucins (such as polymyxin B, etc.) [34]. Considering the drug monitoring in line with clinical practice, the possible effects of these related drugs and common physiological substances on the detection of TIGE were investigated to evaluate the selectivity of this method. As shown in Fig. 4A, TIGE can cause significant quenching of the fluorescence of F, N-CDs, while the QE of other interferences was less than 1 / 15 of that of TIGE, suggesting the negligible QE of the interfering substance to F, N-CDs. Moreover, the QE of F, N-CDs by TIGE was almost kept at the same degree with and without the presence of interference (Fig. 4B), also confirming the excellent anti-interference capacity of this method for TIGE detection. The results indicated the comfortable specificity of this method for potential real blood drug monitoring.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs at different TIGE concentrations were detected. With the increase of TIGE (CTIGE), the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs gradually decreased (Fig. 4C), and the QE ((F0-F)/F0) gradually increased and reached a plateau (inset of Fig. 4D). The maximum quenching degree of F, N-CDs caused by TIGE (50 μg/mL) was calculated to be 73.9%. Furthermore, it showed a good linear relationship between QE and CTIGE in the range of 0.005–20 μg/mL. The linear fitting equation was y = 0.02184 CTIGE + 0.03804 (R2 = 0.997) with the lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) of 0.005 μg/mL (RSD was 4.84% with five parallel determinations). Furthermore, TIGE injection was used as the actual sample to evaluate the reliability and real application of the proposed method in drug quality control. The labeling amount of the injection was tested and calculated according to the standard curve shown in Fig. 4D. The labeled amount of TIGE injection was tested to be 101.8% with the RSD of 1.01% (n = 3) (Table S2), which met the requirement of Ch. P 2020 well. The result showed the accuracy and reproducibility of this method for quality control and application potential in the manufacture of TIGE injection with the characteristic of facile operation.

In the process of blood drug concentration monitoring, the detection sensitivity and accuracy in plasma samples should be further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4E, compared with the system in PBS, the influence trend of different concentrations of TIGE on the fluorescence behavior of F, N-CDs in plasma environment was basically the same. When the CTIGE changing in the range of 0.005–20 μg/mL, a good linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and CTIGE was obtained with the fitting equation of y = 0.02197 CTIGE + 0.03884 (R2 = 0.995) (Fig. 4F). And the LLOQ was 0.005 μg/mL with the RSD of 5.03% (n = 5). Comparing the two linear equations in PBS (y = 0.02184 CTIGE + 0.03804) and plasma environment (y = 0.02197 CTIGE + 0.03884), their slopes and intercepts were almost the same. This result showed that this method can effectively eliminate the influence of complex components in plasma samples for the effective detection of TIGE [56]. The main reasons of this result were as follows: (1) the large Stokes shift (135 nm) of F, N-CDs help to reduce the spectral overlap of excitation and emission of fluorophores to weaken the possible self-absorption of F, N-CDs in testing samples; (2) the maximum emission of F, N-CDs at 500 nm may decrease the interference from the mainly blue autofluorescence of biological system [57,58].

The reproducibility and recovery of this method in plasma were discussed to further evaluate the potential of this method for blood drug monitoring. It was evaluated by repeatedly detecting the LLOQ, low concentration and high concentration of TIGE in different human plasma environment for 5 days. As shown in table S3, the intra-day RSD was 0.59–3.41%, the inter-day RSD was 0.72–2.91%, and the spiked recovery of the method was 99.84–106.30%, indicating the high precision and accuracy of the method for TIGE monitoring in plasma.

Compared with the other methods for TIGE detection, as shown in table S4, the developed strategy exhibited comparable analytical performance of sensitivity and linear range. In addition, this method has the characteristics of simple mixing operation, fast determination speed and no need of expensive instruments for the quantitative analysis of TIGE.

The investigation of the PK of TIGE was preliminarily fabricated on the basis of the developed method. At first, the calibration curve of this method for TIGE in rat plasma was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5A, with the increase of CTIGE, the fluorescence intensities of F, N-CDs in rat plasma gradually decreased. Correspondingly, the QE of F, N-CDs gradually increased (inset of Fig. 5B) and exhibited linear relationship with CTIGE in the range of 0.005–20 μg/mL (Fig. 5B). The linear fitting equation was y = 0.02210 CTIGE + 0.03445 (R2 = 0.996), and LLOQ was 0.005 μg/mL with the RSD of 5.61% (n = 5). This slope and intercept were similar to that of the detection of TIGE in PBS and in human plasma, which verified the practicability of this method in animal system. Considering the potentially consumed time in animal experiment, the testing stability of TIGE in rat plasma was evaluated by detecting the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs in rat plasma samples containing TIGE stored at 4 ℃ at different duration. As shown in Fig. 5C, regardless of the concentration of TIGE, the fluorescence intensity of F, N-CDs in rat plasma containing TIGE was stable, suggesting the little impact of storage on the testing performance in rat plasma.

In clinic, TIGE can only be administered intravenously because of the low oral bioavailability. Generally, TIGE is administered by 100 mg load dose, 50 mg q12h maintenance dose or 200 mg load dose, 100 mg q12h maintenance dose, and 30–60 min of intravenous drip each time. According to the clinical medication and the operability of animal experiments, the administration mode of intravenous rapid injection was adopted. That is, TIGE was injected through the tail vein with low dose (1 μg/g), medium dose (2 μg/g) and high dose (4 μg/g) respectively to different groups. According to the analytical performance of TIGE in rat plasma (Fig. 5B), the plasma concentration of TIGE at different time was obtained (Fig. 5D). The drug metabolism process of TIGE in rats after administration was analyzed by non-compartmental model with DAS software, and the main PK parameters of single tail vein injection of TIGE were achieved according to the obtained results from Fig. 5D. The PK parameters were shown in Table 1. Elimination half-life (t1/2) of low, medium and high dose groups were 379.33, 313.38 and 230.89 min respectively, and elimination constant (β) were 0.0019, 0.0023 and 0.0030 mL/min respectively, and the clearance rates (CL) were 0.3684, 0.4813 and 0.5119 mL/min/g respectively, suggesting the dose dependence of TIGE. The results showed a trend of modulated elimination half-life, higher elimination constant and greater clearance rate ascribed to the variable dose of TIGE, which was similar to the reported result that the clearance rate of TIGE in human varied among different individuals with weak trend effect with ascending dose [59,60]. It is reported that the t1/2 of TIGE with different dose is variable. The reason is that it is a secondary PK parameter, which will change with the changes of main parameters, clearance and apparent distribution volume, especially the apparent distribution volume, which may be due to the nonlinear kinetic characteristics in plasma and / or tissue [61]. The apparent distribution volumes (Vd) of the low, medium and high dose groups were 196.72, 216.06 and 170.80 mL/g, respectively, indicating that TIGE was widely distributed in tissues, which far exceeds the plasma volume of rat. In general, the injected TIGE can be rapidly distributed in various tissues and organs, such as gallbladder, abdominal cavity, lung, skin and soft tissue, so as to make the drug concentration at the infection site reach the target as soon as possible [62]. The huge Vd suggested that TIGE could distribute in virtually every body fluid [62], which was in accord with the current clinical application that TIGE can treat pulmonary infection, abdominal infection and skin soft tissue infection. The average residence time (MRT0 - ∞) was 474.32 min at low dose, which was longer than medium dose (359.69 min) and high dose (268.18 min). AUC0 - ∞ under the curve of low, medium and high dose groups were 636.64, 1008.00 and 1840.55 μg·min/mL, respectively. Compared with the t1/2 of TIGE, the testing time was limited, but it still illustrated that AUC of TIGE is obviously dose-dependent, which is consistent with the reported work [63]. In clinical practice, better therapeutic effect can be obtained by formulating the administration scheme according to the PK / PD of antibiotics. As a time-dependent antibiotic with long post antibiotic effect (PAE), the PK / PD parameter of TIGE is the ratio of the area under the drug time curve AUC0–24 to the minimum inhibition concentration (MIC), also noted as AUIC. The AUIC value is obtained by calculating the PK parameters to judge whether the administration scheme of TIGE is individualized and accurate. According to the location and type of infection, researchers have determined the target of TIGE which is called AUIC breakpoint. For example, AUIC breakpoint of complex abdominal infection [64], complex skin and soft tissue infection [65] and hospital acquired pneumonia (HAP) [66,67] should be ≥ 6.96, ≥ 17.9 and ≥ 4.5, respectively. When the drug sensitivity test shows that the MIC of pathogenic bacteria is high, in order to obtain greater AUC, the dosage should be appropriately increased to achieve the effective treatment target [63,67], and the doubling of the first dose can make the TIGE concentration reach the steady state faster [68]. Therefore, the PK / PD compliance rate can be obtained by monitoring the blood concentration of patients with TIGE and calculating the AUIC of different infection types, so as to provide important reference value for optimizing the use of clinical TIGE. Our research properly matched the clinical practice, suggesting the practicability of this method for PK study of TIGE.

Admittedly, although the results of this method obtained specific and accurate PK parameters of TIGE in rat experiments, in-depth analysis was still needed to achieve the establishment of appropriate transformation model from the establishment of animal experimental model to the establishment of PK parameters of TIGE in human for the more appropriate medication guideline.

To sum up, taking green emission F, N-CDs with large Stokes shift as fluorescent probe, a signal-off fluorescence method was established to detect TIGE accurately and reliably on the basis of dual quenching effect of IFE and SQE. This method had been successfully introduced to detect TIGE for injection and the labeled TIGE in human plasma samples. The recovery and reproducibility were satisfactory, indicating that this method had good detection performance and application prospect in drug preparations and clinical blood concentration monitoring. Using healthy SD rats as the experimental object, TIGE in rat plasma was detected by the fluorescence platform constructed through F, N-CDs as probe. The bleeding drug concentration time curve was drawn, and the main PK parameters of TIGE were obtained after a single tail vein injection in rats. It showed that the fluorescence sensor can provide a new method for the concentration monitoring and PK of TIGE in vivo, suggesting the considerable clinical value for the application of F, N-CDs and fluorescence analysis in biomedical field. Furthermore, this work introduced a new insight about the development of special fluorescence strategy in blood drug monitor and the PK study that needed fast and accurate drug detection in ICU patients.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Rouying Cai earned her Master's degree at the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, in 2021. She works as a pharmacist-in-charge in the Department of Pharmacy at Fujian Provincial Geriatric Hospital. Her research focuses on the new pharmaceutical analysis technology in clinical pharmacy.

Chenfang Miao received her BS degree in Pharmaceutical Analysis from Fujian Medical University in 2019. She is currently a MS graduate student in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research interest is pharmaceutical analysis and fluorescence analysis.

Liang Zhang earned his Bachelor's degree at the Department of pharmacy, Kunming Medical University, in 2010. He works as a pharmacist-in-charge in the Department of Pharmacy at Fujian Provincial Geriatric Hospital. His research interest is the application of pharmaceutical analysis in clinical pharmacy.
Yi Zhou is a undergraduate student of the major of Clinical Pharmacy in the School of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. Her research interest is biopharmaceutical analysis.

Yuebin Liu is a chief pharmacist in the Department of Pharmacy at Fuzong Clinical Medical College of Fujian Medical University (900th Hospital of the Joint Logistics Team), People’ s Republic of China. His research focuses on pharmaceutical analysis.

Chen Zheng earned her Bachelor's degree at the School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, in 2007. She works as a deputy professor of pharmacy in the Department of Pharmacy at Fujian Provincial Geriatric Hospital. Her research focuses on clinical pharmacy, especially the rational application of antibiotics.

Wendi Han earned her Master degree at the Department of Pharmacology, Fujian Medical University, in 2011. She is a clinical pharmacist in the Department of Pharmacy, First Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on clinical pharmacokinetics、clinical pharmacology and rational use of drugs.

Zhengjun Huang earned his Master degree at the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Fujian Medical University, in 2012. He is an experimentalist in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. His research focuses on construction and application of nanomaterials in bioanalysis.

Xin Zhou earned her Master degree at the Department of Pharmacy, Second Military Medical University, in 2006. She works as a deputy professor of pharmacy in the Department of Pharmacy at Fuzong Clinical Medical College of Fujian Medical University (900th Hospital of the Joint Logistics Team), People’ s Republic of China. Her research focuses on pharmaceutical analysis, preparation and therapy of Nanomedicines.

Shaohuang Weng earned his Ph.D. degree at the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University, in 2009. He is an Associate professor in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, School of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. From 2016–2017, he did his research about the optical sensor and DNA biosensor in Xiamen University as a visiting scholar. His research focuses on preparation and application of nanomaterials in bioanalysis.
